enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted XMPP/Jabber server with a web-based admin console, REST API plugin, and clustering support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Openfire is a real-time collaboration server that implements the XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) standard, released under the Apache License 2.0. The project began in 2002 as Jive Messenger, was renamed Wildfire in 2005, and became Openfire in 2007 after Jive Software handed it to the community; it is now maintained by the Ignite Realtime Foundation.

Administrators install Openfire on their own Windows, Linux, Unix, or Mac OS X servers and manage it through a browser-based admin console. It supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Firebird, and CockroachCB as backing databases, and its functionality can be extended through an official plugin catalog, including a REST API plugin for programmatic administration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Openfire suits IT teams and developers who need an internal, self-hosted messaging and presence server — for example, organizations that want group chat, one-to-one messaging, and multi-user chat rooms without routing traffic through a third-party service.

features.title: XMPP-standard messaging
features.description: Implements the open XMPP protocol for instant messaging, presence, and multi-user chat rooms.

features.title: Web-based admin console
features.description: Configure users, groups, chat rooms, and server settings through a browser interface, without editing config files directly.

features.title: REST API plugin
features.description: An official plugin exposes a RESTful API (JSON or XML) for managing users, groups, rosters, and chat rooms from external applications.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Extend the server with community and Ignite Realtime plugins covering monitoring, clustering, gateways, and more.

features.title: Multi-database support
features.description: Works with MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Firebird, and CockroachDB as the storage backend.

features.title: Security hardening
features.description: Includes SASL channel binding, TLS/certificate management, and other protocol-level security options.

pros: Mature project with roots going back over two decades
Free and fully self-hosted — no per-user licensing cost
Web admin console makes day-to-day management accessible without CLI work
Extensible via an official plugin catalog and REST API
cons: Requires the operator to handle their own hosting, updates, and TLS certificates
XMPP client apps (for end users) must be installed separately from the server
Community-maintained, so support depends on forums rather than a paid SLA by default

faqs.question: Is Openfire free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Openfire is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 and is free to download, run, and modify.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Openfire run on?
faqs.answer: Openfire runs on Windows, Linux, Unix, and Mac OS X.

faqs.question: Does Openfire have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ignite Realtime publishes an official REST API plugin that lets other systems manage users, groups, and chat rooms over HTTP.

faqs.question: What chat clients work with Openfire?
faqs.answer: Any standards-compliant XMPP client can connect to Openfire, including Converse.js for web-based chat.

use_cases.title: Internal team messaging
use_cases.description: Run a private, self-hosted instant messaging service for an organization instead of relying on a third-party chat provider.

use_cases.title: Custom messaging integrations
use_cases.description: Use the REST API plugin to provision users and manage chat rooms programmatically from internal tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Openfire is a free, open-source, self-hosted XMPP server with a web admin console, REST API plugin, and plugin ecosystem.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, open-source XMPP server designed for easy setup, low resource use, and Lua-based module extensibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Prosody is a modern XMPP communication server started by Matthew Wild in August 2008, with its first public release in December 2008. It is released under the MIT License and written in Lua, which keeps its codebase compact and makes it straightforward to write custom modules.

Prosody is designed to be simple to install and configure while still supporting server-to-server federation with other XMPP-compatible services. It powers, or forms part of the backend for, several well-known projects, including Jitsi Meet and Snikket.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Prosody fits sysadmins and developers who want a lean, self-hosted XMPP server for personal, family, or organizational messaging, or as a federation-capable backend component for another product.

features.title: Standard XMPP server
features.description: Implements core XMPP messaging, presence, and multi-user chat over the open Jabber/XMPP protocol.

features.title: Server-to-server federation
features.description: Connects with other Prosody installations and any XMPP-compatible service for cross-server messaging.

features.title: Module system
features.description: A modular architecture written in Lua lets developers add or customize functionality and prototype new XEP extensions.

features.title: Low resource footprint
features.description: Designed to run efficiently on modest hardware, making it viable for small self-hosted deployments.

pros: Simple to install and configure compared to some other XMPP server implementations
MIT-licensed and completely free to self-host
Powers production services like Jitsi Meet and Snikket, showing real-world scale
Modular Lua codebase is approachable for writing custom extensions
cons: Windows support is deprecated; it's best run on Linux/BSD systems
Requires comfort with server administration and config files
No official hosted/managed version — self-hosting is the only option

faqs.question: Is Prosody free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Prosody is open-source software under the MIT License with no licensing cost.

faqs.question: What platforms does Prosody support?
faqs.answer: Prosody runs on Linux, macOS, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, and NetBSD. Windows support is deprecated.

faqs.question: Can Prosody talk to other XMPP servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports server-to-server federation, allowing users on different Prosody or XMPP servers to message each other.

use_cases.title: Personal or family messaging server
use_cases.description: Run a small, private XMPP server for a household or group of friends who want an alternative to centralized chat apps.

use_cases.title: Backend for other products
use_cases.description: Use Prosody as the underlying messaging server for a larger product, as Jitsi Meet and Snikket do.
use_cases.audience: Developers building messaging or video products

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Prosody IM is a free, open-source, self-hosted XMPP server known for easy setup, low resource use, and modular Lua extensibility.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, privacy-focused group messaging built on XMPP, with dedicated iOS and Android apps and no ads or tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Snikket is an open-source messaging platform first previewed in early 2020 as a self-hosted alternative to centralized apps like WhatsApp or Telegram. It bundles existing open-source XMPP components — the Prosody server, an Android client based on Conversations, and an iOS client based on Siskin IM — into a package that's easy for a non-expert to run.

Rather than a single company controlling all accounts, Snikket is designed as a federated network: anyone can run their own server (or use a Snikket-hosted instance) while still being able to message people on other Snikket or XMPP servers. In March 2024 the project launched Snikket Hosting, an optional paid managed-hosting service for people who don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Snikket targets families, friend groups, clubs, and small organizations that want private group messaging and calling without their data being monetized, whether they self-host or pay for managed hosting.

features.title: Group messaging and calling
features.description: Supports one-to-one and group chats plus audio/video calling within the mobile apps.

features.title: "Circles" for organizing contacts
features.description: Lets admins group members into circles so people can find and message each other without sharing phone numbers or address books.

features.title: Native iOS and Android apps
features.description: Dedicated open-source apps for both major mobile platforms, derived from established XMPP clients (Siskin IM and Conversations).

features.title: Self-hosted or managed server
features.description: Run your own Snikket server for full control, or subscribe to Snikket Hosting for a managed instance.

features.title: No ads or data sale
features.description: The project states it does not show ads or sell user data on self-hosted or hosted instances.

pros: Purpose-built for non-technical users compared to raw XMPP server setup
Native mobile apps for both iOS and Android
Federated design avoids lock-in to a single company's servers
Self-hosting remains free and fully open source
cons: iOS app is currently English-only, unlike the more widely translated Android app
Managed hosting has no free tier — only a 14-day trial
Smaller ecosystem and userbase than mainstream chat apps

faqs.question: Is Snikket free?
faqs.answer: The Snikket server and apps are free, open-source software you can self-host. Snikket Hosting, an optional managed hosting service, is a paid product starting around $6/month depending on region, with a 14-day trial.

faqs.question: What apps does Snikket offer?
faqs.answer: Snikket provides open-source iOS and Android apps, available through the App Store, Google Play, and F-Droid.

faqs.question: Can Snikket users message people on other servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, because Snikket is built on XMPP, users can message people on other Snikket instances or compatible XMPP servers.

use_cases.title: Family or friend group chat
use_cases.description: Set up a private messaging space for a household or friend group without going through a big tech company.

use_cases.title: Small organization communication
use_cases.description: Give a club or small organization its own private messaging network with circles for different topics or teams.

features: Full server and app source available
Run on your own hardware or VPS
No user limit set by Snikket
Managed instance for up to 10 active users

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Snikket is a free, open-source, self-hosted XMPP messaging platform with native iOS and Android apps and no ads or tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Modular, Java-based XMPP/Jabber server built to scale from small deployments to millions of concurrent users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tigase XMPP Server is a Java-based, open-source XMPP/Jabber server started by Artur Hefczyc in October 2004. It's released under the AGPL-3.0 license, with Tigase, Inc. also offering a commercial license for organizations that can't use AGPL terms.

The project emphasizes performance and low footprint: its core binary is under 3MB, it can run with as little as 10MB of RAM for basic setups, and the vendor reports deployments handling large volumes of traffic with near-linear scalability across clustered nodes. Being JVM-based, it can be deployed on-premises or on any cloud provider that supports Java.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Tigase suits engineering teams building large-scale or high-uptime messaging infrastructure — from self-hosted community servers up to enterprise deployments needing clustering and detailed runtime metrics.

features.title: Modular Java architecture
features.description: A component-based design lets operators enable only the modules they need.

features.title: Clustering and load balancing
features.description: Supports distributed deployments with near-linear scalability and automatic cluster reconfiguration.

features.title: Administration tooling
features.description: Includes command-line tools, a web UI, and runtime performance metrics for monitoring the server.

features.title: Small footprint
features.description: Core binary under 3MB and capable of running with minimal RAM for lightweight deployments.

features.title: Dual licensing
features.description: Free under AGPL-3.0, with a commercial license option from Tigase, Inc. for closed-source use.

pros: Designed and tested for very large-scale deployments
Low resource requirements for smaller instances
Commercial support available through Tigase, Inc. for organizations that need it
Long track record dating back to 2004
cons: AGPL-3.0 licensing requires attention to compliance for SaaS/hosted use cases
Java-based stack may be heavier to operate than lighter alternatives for small hobby deployments
Commercial pricing is not published and requires contacting Tigase, Inc.

faqs.question: Is Tigase free?
faqs.answer: The core Tigase XMPP Server is free and open source under AGPL-3.0. Tigase, Inc. also sells a commercial license for cases where AGPL terms aren't suitable.

faqs.question: What platforms does Tigase run on?
faqs.answer: As a Java application, it runs anywhere a JVM is supported, including on-premises servers and major cloud providers.

faqs.question: Does Tigase support clustering?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's built for distributed deployments with load balancing and near-linear scalability.

use_cases.title: Large-scale messaging infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run a self-hosted XMPP backend intended to serve very large numbers of concurrent users.

use_cases.title: Embedded messaging in a product
use_cases.description: License Tigase commercially to embed XMPP messaging in a closed-source product.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tigase is an open-source, Java-based XMPP server built for large-scale, self-hosted messaging deployments, with AGPL and commercial licensing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, browser-based XMPP chat client that can run as a full-page app, embedded widget, or overlay on any site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Converse.js is a client-side XMPP chat application written in JavaScript, created by JC Brand starting in 2012 and released under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPLv2). Because it runs entirely in the browser, it can connect to any standards-compliant XMPP server, including Openfire, Prosody, and ejabberd, without vendor lock-in.

It supports direct messages, group chats (multi-user chatrooms), OMEMO end-to-end encryption, HTTP file upload, message archiving, and translations into more than 30 languages. Converse can be deployed as a full-page web app, embedded as a widget in an existing site, or overlaid as a chat popup, and it has been integrated into projects such as PeerTube, WordPress, and Roundcube.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Converse.js is aimed at developers who need to add real-time chat to a website or product and want a self-hosted, open-source client rather than a proprietary embedded widget.

features.title: Multiple deployment modes
features.description: Use it as a standalone full-page app, an embeddable widget, or an overlay chat window on an existing site.

features.title: OMEMO end-to-end encryption
features.description: Supports OMEMO for encrypted one-to-one and group conversations.

features.title: Multi-user chatrooms
features.description: Supports XMPP MUC group chats alongside direct messaging.

features.title: Broad server compatibility
features.description: Works with any XMPP server, including Openfire, Prosody, and ejabberd.

features.title: Wide translation coverage
features.description: Localized into more than 30 languages by the community.

features.title: File sharing
features.description: Supports HTTP file upload for sharing images and documents in chat.

pros: No server-side component required — runs entirely client-side against any XMPP server
Flexible embedding options (widget, overlay, or full app)
MPLv2 license avoids vendor lock-in
Already integrated into several other open-source projects
cons: Requires a separate XMPP server (e.g. Prosody or Openfire) to actually send messages
End users need some XMPP account/server setup, which is less turnkey than closed SaaS chat widgets
Official commercial support is provided by a separate consultancy (Opkode), not the core project

faqs.question: Is Converse.js free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open-source software under the Mozilla Public License 2.0.

faqs.question: Does Converse.js need its own server?
faqs.answer: No, it's a client only — it connects to any existing XMPP server such as Prosody, Openfire, or ejabberd.

faqs.question: Can I embed Converse.js in my website?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports being embedded as a widget or chat overlay, in addition to running as a standalone app.

use_cases.title: Add chat to a website
use_cases.description: Embed Converse.js as a widget or overlay to give visitors real-time chat backed by an XMPP server.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team or community chat UI
use_cases.description: Pair Converse.js with a self-hosted XMPP server like Prosody or Openfire for a fully self-hosted messaging stack.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Converse.js is a free, open-source, browser-based XMPP chat client that works with any XMPP server and embeds into any website.

name: Libervia
skip_reason: Libervia is the current name of the Salut à Toi project (the project was renamed from Salut à Toi to Libervia as of version 0.8; the nonprofit association behind it still uses the name Salut à Toi). It is the same software already covered by the 'salut-a-toi' entry in this batch, and the listed website (https://repos.goffi.org/libervia-web) returns a 404, with the live, first-party site being salut-a-toi.org instead.

enrichment.short_description: Decentralized, XMPP-based communication and collaboration suite (now renamed Libervia) with web, desktop, mobile, and CLI clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salut à Toi is a decentralized communication and collaboration project built on the XMPP protocol, maintained by a French nonprofit (association loi 1901) led by developer Goffi. As of version 0.8, the software itself was renamed Libervia — the association behind the project keeps the name Salut à Toi, but the project and its interfaces are now branded Libervia.

Most of the codebase is released under AGPL v3+. It goes beyond instant messaging to cover blogging (via Pubsub nodes), calendar events, photo albums, decentralized task lists and grocery lists, and file sharing, with interfaces for the web (Libervia Web), desktop, an experimental Android app, a CLI, and a terminal UI. It also offers an ActivityPub gateway to bridge XMPP conversations with the fediverse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It suits users and developers who want a federated, self-hostable alternative to mainstream messaging and social platforms, and who need more than chat — such as shared calendars, blogs, or task lists — built on an open protocol they can run themselves.

features.title: End-to-end encrypted messaging
features.description: Supports OMEMO and OpenPGP encryption for one-to-one and group conversations.

features.title: Multi-user chat and calls
features.description: Group chat (MUC) plus one-on-one and multi-participant audio/video calls using peer-to-peer or SFU routing.

features.title: Pubsub-based blogging and calendars
features.description: Publish blog posts and calendar events with RSVP over XMPP Pubsub nodes.

features.title: Decentralized lists
features.description: Shared task lists, tickets, and grocery lists that sync across a user's devices and contacts.

features.title: ActivityPub gateway
features.description: Bridges XMPP conversations and content with fediverse platforms that speak ActivityPub.

features.title: Multiple client interfaces
features.description: Available as a web app (Libervia Web), desktop client, experimental Android app, CLI, and terminal UI.

pros: Goes well beyond chat into blogging, calendars, and file/task sharing on a single decentralized protocol
Multiple client types (web, desktop, CLI, TUI) for different workflows
AGPL-licensed and self-hostable
Built-in bridge to the ActivityPub fediverse
cons: Android support is still experimental and there's no stable iOS app
Smaller community and documentation base than mainstream XMPP clients
Project rename (Salut à Toi to Libervia) can make it harder to find current resources

faqs.question: Is Salut à Toi the same as Libervia?
faqs.answer: Yes. As of version 0.8, the project itself was renamed Libervia; the nonprofit association behind it retains the name Salut à Toi.

faqs.question: Is it free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, nearly all of the codebase is released under AGPL v3+ and is free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does it only do chat?
faqs.answer: No, it also supports Pubsub-based blogging, calendars, photo albums, task/grocery lists, and file sharing on top of XMPP.

use_cases.title: Federated personal communication
use_cases.description: Use it as a decentralized alternative to mainstream chat and social apps, hosted on your own or a trusted XMPP server.

use_cases.title: Lightweight collaboration
use_cases.description: Coordinate shared calendars, task lists, and blogs with a group over XMPP instead of separate SaaS tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salut à Toi (now Libervia) is a free, open-source, decentralized XMPP suite for messaging, blogging, calendars, and file sharing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source management platform for Community Supported Agriculture groups, covering members, deliveries, and billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ACP Admin — also published under the name CSA Admin, since "ACP" (Agriculture Contractuelle de Proximité) is the French-language term for Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) — is a web application originally built by developer Thibaud Guillaume-Gentil to manage Rage de Vert, a CSA group in Neuchâtel, Switzerland. It has been used by organizations since 2014 and now serves more than 30 organizations across Switzerland, Germany, and the Netherlands, managing over 140,000 basket deliveries a year.

The source is released under the O'Saasy License, which permits free self-hosting for any purpose but reserves the right to resell the software as a competing hosted service to the original author. The maintainer also offers a managed hosting option with GDPR/nLPD-compliant infrastructure, backups, and updates included.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's built for CSA/ACP organizers — associations, cooperatives, or small businesses running subscription-based produce delivery — who need to manage members, waitlists, delivery schedules, invoicing, and a supplementary online shop in one system.

features.title: Member and waitlist management
features.description: Handles member registration, subscription management, and GDPR-compliant waitlists.

features.title: Delivery scheduling
features.description: Supports flexible delivery frequencies and basket content tracking.

features.title: Automated invoicing
features.description: Generates invoices with QR codes and tracks payments, including EBICS integration for Swiss bank reconciliation.

features.title: Annual renewal workflows
features.description: Simplifies the yearly process of renewing member subscriptions.

features.title: Online shop add-on
features.description: Sells supplementary products alongside regular baskets, with inventory tracking.

features.title: Member communication tools
features.description: Customizable email and newsletter tools for keeping members informed.

pros: Purpose-built for CSA/ACP operations rather than generic e-commerce
Free to self-host under the O'Saasy License
Managed hosting option removes the need to run infrastructure yourself
Proven at scale with 140,000+ baskets managed annually across multiple organizations
cons: Managed hosting pricing is usage-based (percentage of invoiced amount), which can be harder to budget than a flat fee
The O'Saasy License restricts reselling it as a competing hosted service
Maintained largely by a single developer rather than a company or large team

faqs.question: Is ACP Admin free?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting the software is free under the O'Saasy License. The maintainer's managed hosting service charges 1% of the amount invoiced through the platform per year, capped at 2,400 CHF/EUR, with no setup fees.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ACP Admin and CSA Admin?
faqs.answer: They are the same software; "ACP" is the French term used in French-speaking regions, while "CSA" is the English term for Community Supported Agriculture.

faqs.question: Can I self-host ACP Admin?
faqs.answer: Yes, the O'Saasy License explicitly permits free self-hosting; the maintainer's managed hosting is an optional paid alternative.

use_cases.title: Running a CSA/ACP subscription program
use_cases.description: Manage member subscriptions, delivery schedules, and basket invoicing for a produce cooperative.

use_cases.title: Multi-organization farm networks
use_cases.description: Coordinate several affiliated CSA groups on shared or self-hosted infrastructure.

features: Full source code available on GitHub

pricing_plans.price: 1% of amount invoiced per year, capped at 2,400 CHF/EUR

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ACP Admin (CSA Admin) is open-source software for managing Community Supported Agriculture groups: members, deliveries, and invoicing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source ordering and billing platform for food cooperatives, organic shops, and farm stores.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FoodCoopShop is an open-source web application, started by developer Mario Rothauer in 2014, for managing regional food orders from direct marketers. It's built on CakePHP and MySQL and is free to download and self-host, with Docker support for local development.

The software gives producers a dedicated login to manage their own product listings while giving cooperative members a shared ordering interface with support for different delivery schedules and an automated credit/balance billing system. According to the project's own site, over 50 initiatives are currently using it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at food cooperatives, organic stores (Bioläden), and farm shops (Hofläden) — mainly in German-speaking regions — that need collective ordering and producer coordination without building custom software.

features.title: Collective ordering
features.description: Members place orders from a shared catalog sourced from multiple producers.

features.title: Producer login area
features.description: Gives producers direct access to manage their own products and availability.

features.title: Flexible delivery schedules
features.description: Supports different delivery/pickup schedules per cooperative.

features.title: Credit/balance billing
features.description: Automates member billing through a running credit and balance system rather than per-order invoicing.

features.title: Docker-based dev environment
features.description: Ships with Docker tooling to simplify setting up a local development or self-hosted instance.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hostable under CakePHP/MySQL
Purpose-built for the specific workflow of food co-ops with multiple producers
Producer-facing login reduces manual coordination work
Active maintenance with at least one release per year
cons: Primarily documented and used in German-speaking markets, though English is supported
Self-hosting requires PHP/MySQL server administration skills
Maintained mainly by its original developer rather than a large team

faqs.question: Is FoodCoopShop free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source. Paid support and hosting services are available separately from the creator.

faqs.question: What is FoodCoopShop built with?
faqs.answer: It's built on CakePHP and MySQL, with Docker support for setting up a development environment.

faqs.question: Who is FoodCoopShop for?
faqs.answer: Food cooperatives, organic shops (Bioläden), and farm stores (Hofläden) that need collective ordering and producer coordination.

use_cases.title: Cooperative food ordering
use_cases.description: Run a shared online ordering system for a food cooperative sourcing from multiple local producers.

use_cases.title: Farm/organic shop management
use_cases.description: Give a farm shop or organic store an online ordering and billing system tailored to direct-marketing sales.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FoodCoopShop is free, open-source software for food cooperatives and farm shops to manage collective ordering and producer billing.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source web app for running a non-profit food cooperative: product catalog, ordering, accounting, and job scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Foodsoft is an open-source, web-based application for running a non-profit food cooperative — a group of members who buy directly from suppliers of their own choosing, effectively operating a collective do-it-yourself supermarket. It's maintained on GitHub under the foodcoops organization and has a long history within the German and European food-coop community.

The software covers the core operational needs of a coop: maintaining a shared product catalog, taking member orders online, tracking accounting/balances, and scheduling the volunteer jobs members take on to keep the coop running. It can be self-hosted, and the project also lists third-party "Foodsoft Hosting" options for coops that don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Foodsoft is built for member-run, non-profit food cooperatives that need to coordinate ordering, accounting, and volunteer job scheduling across a group of members rather than a traditional retail storefront.

features.title: Shared product catalog
features.description: Maintains a coop-wide catalog of products sourced from chosen suppliers.

features.title: Online member ordering
features.description: Members place and track their own orders through the web app.

features.title: Coop accounting
features.description: Tracks member balances and coop-level accounting tied to orders.

features.title: Volunteer job scheduling
features.description: Coordinates the shifts and tasks members take on as part of running the coop.

features.title: Self-hosted or hosted deployment
features.description: Can be run on your own infrastructure or through third-party Foodsoft Hosting providers.

pros: Free and open source, purpose-built for non-profit food coop operations
Covers ordering, accounting, and job scheduling in one system rather than several tools
Long-running project with an established community of food coops using it
Hosting options exist for coops that don't want to self-host
cons: Documentation and community are strongest in German-speaking regions
Self-hosting requires Ruby on Rails server administration knowledge
Volunteer-maintained pace of development can vary over time

faqs.question: Is Foodsoft free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Foodsoft is open-source software and free to self-host. Some third-party providers also offer paid Foodsoft Hosting for coops that don't want to manage a server.

faqs.question: What does Foodsoft manage?
faqs.answer: It manages a coop's product catalog, member ordering, accounting, and volunteer job scheduling.

faqs.question: Who maintains Foodsoft?
faqs.answer: It's maintained as a community open-source project on GitHub under the foodcoops organization.

use_cases.title: Running a member-owned food coop
use_cases.description: Coordinate product ordering, accounting, and volunteer scheduling for a non-profit, member-run food cooperative.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Foodsoft is free, open-source software for running a non-profit food cooperative: catalog, ordering, accounting, and job scheduling.
